D6677 Reapproval Year | 2022 | Year | 2022 |\n\n*Table 1: Coating Adhesion Test Standards and Measurements*\n\n\n## ASTM D4541 Protocols and Pull-Off Standardization\n\nASTM D4541 serves as the primary framework for quantifying pull-off strength, distinguishing itself from qualitative alternatives through specific protocol requirements [CITE:ev_11dcbcb0106cc0e2]. This method is recognized as the standard test method for pull-off strength of coatings using portable testers [CITE:ev_5c51e91bce267103]. Unlike tape-based methods, the pull-off test quantitatively measures adhesion according to ISO 4624 or ASTM D4541 [CITE:ev_1df7fc0cbf4a95af]. The distinction between quantitative and qualitative evaluation is critical when comparing epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials, as performance metrics differ significantly across these chemistries. The Cross-Cut Test provides qualitative evaluation while the Pull-Off Method provides quantitative evaluation of coating adhesion [CITE:ev_84a08742318ed15d]. This divergence influences material selection, particularly for structural applications where precise bond strength data is required. ASTM D4541 and BS EN ISO 4624 describe pull-off adhesion testing standards for protective coatings [CITE:ev_befc6654b3c8f5ad]. Conversely, ASTM D3359 outlines standardized test methods for evaluating adhesion of coatings to metallic substrates using pressure-sensitive tape [CITE:ev_815bc543dbac8f04]. The tape test is qualitative and cannot precisely assess quantitatively the actual strength of the adhesion [CITE:ev_389d441cc3fe3ba4]. Consequently, for polymer coatings where tensile integrity is paramount, pull-off protocols offer superior data fidelity.\n\nTable 1 compares the primary adhesion testing methodologies referenced in the evidence, highlighting differences in standards, measurement types, and output units.\n\n| Test Method | Primary Standard | Measurement Type | Output Unit/Scale |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| Pull-Off | ASTM D4541 | Quantitative | Tensile Force [CITE:ev_e7dcecbdf7714908] |\n| Pull-Off | ISO 4624 | Quantitative | Tensile Force [CITE:ev_7267f03f92de9267] |\n| Cross-Cut | ASTM D3359 | Qualitative | 0B to 5B Scale [CITE:ev_d0d69c4b450fa1e3] |\n| Cross-Cut | ISO 2409 | Qualitative | Detachment Evaluation [CITE:ev_067cc0abd42529d2] |\n\nThe data indicates that pull-off testing follows ISO 4624, ASTM D4541, and D7234 standards for evaluating coating peel strength [CITE:ev_0ffc84130522e5ce]. However, ASTM D7234 defines the standard test method for pull-off adhesion strength on concrete using portable testers [CITE:ev_3a7d481c74614432]. This creates a distinction between general coating standards and substrate-specific protocols. ASTM D4541 specifies procedures for measuring pull-off strength of coatings using portable adhesion testers [CITE:ev_11dcbcb0106cc0e2]. While D4541 is broad, D7234 is specialized, suggesting that concrete applications require distinct validation compared to metallic substrates used for epoxy or urethane systems. ASTM D4541 is the standard test method for pull-off strength of coatings using portable testers [CITE:ev_5c51e91bce267103]. The existence of multiple standards necessitates careful selection based on the substrate material to ensure regulatory compliance.\n\nA significant conflict exists regarding the fundamental nature of the data produced by these tests. One perspective states that the pull-off adhesion test measures the strength of the bond between a coating and the surface [CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00]. Conversely, conflicting evidence suggests the test method does not measure fundamental adhesion strength of the bond between coating and substrate [CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d]. This discrepancy implies that while pull-off tests provide quantitative force data, they may reflect cohesive failure within the coating or adhesive rather than pure interfacial adhesion. Traditional adhesion tests like ISO/JIS cross-hatch and ASTM tape tests only evaluate final adhesion strength without explaining adhesion mechanisms [CITE:ev_60e99e00ac021116]. Therefore, while ASTM D4541 provides numerical values, these values might represent a combination of adhesive and cohesive strengths. Adhesion pull-off strength testing determines the overall quality and integrity of an installed coating system [CITE:ev_c357968c8f3b130a]. For the research question comparing epoxy, urethane, and silicone, this means pull-off data indicates system integrity but may not isolate material-specific interfacial chemistry performance.\n\nMaterial suitability further differentiates these protocols. The cross-cut test is especially suitable for thin layers on rigid substrates like metal or plastic [CITE:ev_bb2ddf4927283db1]. In contrast, the test method was originally developed for ductile coatings applied to metal substrates and not for coatings on plastics [CITE:ev_5b8a012a381ea628]. This limitation is critical for silicone and urethane coatings which may exhibit different elasticity compared to epoxies. Cross hatch tape testing is intended for laboratory use on coatings under 5 mils thickness [CITE:ev_c8b75c8d91e7faea]. Thicker polymer systems, common in protective urethane applications, may exceed this constraint, necessitating pull-off methods. ASTM D3359 is widely recognized standard for evaluating coating adhesion on metal substrates using tape test method [CITE:ev_59cf9ed236eb49bd]. However, ASTM D3359 is used as an adhesion standard despite not being designed for conformal coatings [CITE:ev_b7b6a159d5d95e2b]. This misapplication risk underscores the need for strict adherence to ASTM D4541 for thicker or non-metallic systems.\n\nSpecific performance metrics vary by material and substrate combination. Most concrete coating adhesion pull-off strength testing exhibits substrate failure in the 400-500 psi range [CITE:ev_983d2ca05a339214]. Hydroxyapatite coatings showed repeatable adhesion strength of 3.4 \u00b1 1.0 MPa across both ASTM test methods [CITE:ev_5bd33c7a02d9cd28]. These variations highlight that absolute strength values are material-dependent. Pull-off strength important parameter when selecting polymer coating systems [CITE:ev_0242e623812a6cb7]. For silicone materials, adhesion test must be conducted prior to estimating coating restoration project per Inland adhesion testing procedures [CITE:ev_61c41c390dad6a70]. Silicone contamination can impact bond performance, as the majority of bond systems underwent tensile adhesion strength testing using a button-to-plate configuration [CITE:ev_e552dd2b19ec130e]. This suggests that for silicone, pull-off configurations must account for potential contamination effects on the interface.\n\nRegulatory compliance extends beyond ASTM to international standards. ISO 4624 is referenced as a global standard for pull-off adhesion testing compliance [CITE:ev_a9eaff2972823a42]. The pull-off test is standardized by ISO 4624:2023 and is widely used to quantify adhesion [CITE:ev_7267f03f92de9267]. Additionally, JIS K 5600-5-6: 1999 specifies the Cross-Cut Test method for coating adhesion evaluation under Japanese Industrial Standards [CITE:ev_38e4b21684c9f29c]. A conflict arises regarding JIS standards, as evidence indicates JIS K 5600-5-7: 2014 specifies the pull-off method as coating tensile adhesion test for quantitative evaluation [CITE:ev_389eb7da7cde2ff8]. This contrasts with the 1999 cross-cut specification. The newer 2014 standard aligns with the quantitative trend seen in ASTM D4541, suggesting a shift toward tensile measurement in regulatory frameworks. ISO 2409 specifies the cross-cut test method for evaluating adhesion of coatings on sheet metal parts [CITE:ev_067cc0abd42529d2]. Compliance with these varied standards requires testing laboratories to maintain equipment capable of both tensile and tape-based evaluations.\n\nASTM D4541 Standard Test Method for Pull-Off Strength requires portable adhesion tester apparatus [CITE:ev_469510656043102c]. The ASTM D4541 Pull-Off Test measures tensile pull-off force required to detach dolly adhesively bonded to coating from substrate [CITE:ev_e7dcecbdf7714908]. This mechanical requirement differentiates it from peel tests, where Peel testing is suitable for designs where bonded edges experience prying or bending [CITE:ev_1be768df70c46a07]. Peel testing methods include T-peel and 90-degree peel tests standardized under ASTM D1876 and D6862 [CITE:ev_16c2d5e21aa178ca]. While peel tests address specific stress modes, pull-off tests address normal stress. ASTM D4541 pull-off test is a fundamental standard for measuring adhesive strength on metallic substrates [CITE:ev_8519c66c1725a078]. For epoxy adhesives, toughened epoxy adhesive Bondmaster E3334 was tested with ATBN reactive liquid polymer toughening agent [CITE:ev_f8d4f44f70298eeb]. The performance of such modified epoxies under ASTM D4541 protocols would differ from unmodified systems, necessitating specific calibration.\n\nScoring systems also vary between methods. Adhesion is rated by inspecting the cut area for coating removal relative to classifications 0B through 5B [CITE:ev_d23f5ac9162865a3]. Method A results assessed on 0A-5A scale where 5A indicates no peeling and 0A indicates complete removal [CITE:ev_5311f47cd26b20c1]. These qualitative scales contrast with the continuous numerical data from pull-off tests. Cross-cut adhesion test evaluation ranges from 0B representing complete detachment to 5B representing no detachment [CITE:ev_d0d69c4b450fa1e3]. The FAS-CeO2/PDMS coating achieved cross-cut adhesion classification of level 2 corresponding to 4B rating [CITE:ev_a02281a13423c35e]. This specific data point illustrates how qualitative ratings map to material performance. However, independent laboratories have noted differences of up to 50 percent in tape adhesion variability over time [CITE:ev_ce3124033536bdb1]. This variability reinforces the preference for ASTM D4541 in high-stakes applications where precision is required.\n\nSite adhesion testing must be verified according to criteria on page 35 to confirm proper surface preparation procedures [CITE:ev_97840fd9469c02ca]. Field adhesion test recommended when laboratory test cannot be performed on-site [CITE:ev_f569492a9905b1d9]. ASTM-D-4541 standard may be used for portable adhesion tester in laboratory or field environment [CITE:ev_eaacf433db8aa353]. This flexibility supports the use of pull-off methods for in-situ verification of epoxy, urethane, and silicone coatings after application. Mode of failure and pull-off pressure confirm proper surface preparation and coating application [CITE:ev_399c2dba04cb99b8]. ASTM D4541 standard states scoring around the fixture violates the fundamental test criterion for un [CITE:ev_82fc33f498c9daa4]. Adherence to these procedural constraints is necessary to validate the quantitative data generated.\n\n**Key Findings**\n*   ASTM D4541 and ISO 4624 provide quantitative tensile force measurements, whereas ASTM D3359 and ISO 2409 offer qualitative scale-based evaluations [CITE:ev_1df7fc0cbf4a95af], [CITE:ev_d0d69c4b450fa1e3].\n*   Conflicting evidence exists on whether pull-off tests measure fundamental adhesion strength or overall system integrity, suggesting results may include cohesive failure components [CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00], [CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d].\n*   Substrate specificity is critical, with ASTM D7234 designated for concrete and ASTM D4541 for general coatings, impacting test selection for polymer systems [CITE:ev_3a7d481c74614432], [CITE:ev_11dcbcb0106cc0e2].\n*   Cross-cut methods are limited to coatings under 5 mils thickness and rigid substrates, restricting their use for thicker urethane or silicone applications [CITE:ev_c8b75c8d91e7faea], [CITE:ev_bb2ddf4927283db1].\n*   Variability in tape adhesion testing can reach 50 percent, supporting the use of pull-off protocols for higher precision in regulatory compliance [CITE:ev_ce3124033536bdb1], [CITE:ev_5c51e91bce267103].\n\n## Peel Test Variations Including 90-Degree and T-Peel Configurations\n\nPeel test variations offer distinct mechanical insights for flexible polymer coatings compared to pull-off standardization protocols. Peel testing functions as a critical methodology for evaluating the bond strength between two materials, particularly where bonded edges experience prying or bending forces [CITE:ev_1be768df70c46a07]. Unlike pull-off adhesion testing which applies tensile force perpendicular to the substrate to measure coating resistance to separation [CITE:ev_f99cba7616963c64], peel tests measure the force required to strip a flexible coating from a substrate [CITE:ev_6e9c26b6c7722bbe]. This distinction is vital for the research question comparing adhesion testing methods across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials, as the mechanical loading mode significantly influences failure modes. Pull-off adhesion testing measures the strength of the bond between a coating and the surface [CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00]. Tape and pull-off tests are common methods for evaluating coating adhesion to substrates [CITE:ev_fee4deed784b02c8].\n\nThe configuration of the peel test dictates the stress distribution at the peel front. Different peel test types include 90-degree peel, 180-degree peel, T-peel, floating roller peel, loop tack strength test, and Double Cantilever Beam test [CITE:ev_918f752bd4fdc4a8]. The 90 Degree Peel Test specifically measures peel strength of adhesives on semi-rigid or rigid substrates [CITE:ev_91884037f7462af0]. In contrast, the 180\u00b0 peel strength test involves bonding two materials, stripping part of the material, and applying force at a 180\u00b0 angle [CITE:ev_b2fcbc49e7f4e906]. For flexible materials bonded to each other, the T-Peel Test is designed to measure the force required to separate two flexible materials that have been bonded [CITE:ev_8358eea47f38ee45]. These variations allow analysts to select the geometry that best mimics the service conditions of the polymer coating. Peel tests measure adhesive strength by pulling one material away from another at a specific angle and speed [CITE:ev_f0f1594bc5d9dd20].\n\n**Table 1: Comparison of Peel Test Configurations and Standards**\n\n| Configuration | Primary Standard | Substrate Suitability | Measurement Unit |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| 90-Degree Peel | ASTM D6862 [CITE:ev_16c2d5e21aa178ca] | Semi-rigid or rigid [CITE:ev_91884037f7462af0] | Force per width [CITE:ev_44fea46094d83d2a] |\n| 180-Degree Peel | ISO 8510-2 [CITE:ev_75d316de9669a24a] | Flexible-bonded-to-rigid [CITE:ev_75d316de9669a24a] | N/mm or lb/in [CITE:ev_44fea46094d83d2a] |\n| T-Peel | ASTM D1876 [CITE:ev_16c2d5e21aa178ca] | Thin-gauge metal adherends [CITE:ev_e3a5ffd440e5e83a] | Force per width [CITE:ev_44fea46094d83d2a] |\n| Cross-Cut | ISO 2409 [CITE:ev_067cc0abd42529d2] | Thin coatings on rigid substrates [CITE:ev_3550bf7b3e0ae5a8] | Classification 0B-5B [CITE:ev_d0d69c4b450fa1e3] |\n\nPeel strength is calculated using force applied during peeling process divided by film width [CITE:ev_1ae4530e37be2c91]. Peel strength is usually reported in units of force per width such as N/mm or lb/in [CITE:ev_44fea46094d83d2a]. This quantitative output contrasts with the Cross-Cut Test which classifies adhesion results into six levels according to the degree of peeling after tape removal [CITE:ev_cd6ffc422440dd18]. The Cross-Cut Test provides qualitative evaluation while Pull-Off Method provides quantitative evaluation of coating adhesion [CITE:ev_84a08742318ed15d]. Traditional adhesion tests like ISO/JIS cross-hatch and ASTM tape tests only evaluate final adhesion strength without explaining adhesion mechanisms [CITE:ev_60e99e00ac021116]. Pull-off test data provides quantitative indication of bond tensile strength [CITE:ev_448284e198da9b99]. Key peel test metrics include initial peak force, average force across entire seal, and peel strength [CITE:ev_9ae74d816a7eacc6]. Peel test values represent an average between 5 and 35mm of peel for each of two tests [CITE:ev_e84426d0da8ec986]. This granularity allows for more nuanced performance tracking than the binary pass/fail nature of some tape tests.\n\nA significant methodological conflict exists regarding the fundamental nature of these measurements. One source states that the pull-off adhesion test measures the strength of the bond between a coating and the surface [CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00]. Another source claims that the test method does not measure fundamental adhesion strength of the bond between coating and substrate [CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d]. This disagreement highlights a critical trade-off in adhesion testing: practical bond strength evaluation versus theoretical interfacial energy measurement. The position that tests measure practical bond strength has stronger support across the evidence base, as multiple sources define these methods by their ability to quantify resistance to separation [CITE:ev_057ad7d7fadf39c2]. The pull-off device provides values for the peel stress, which not only allows a quick determination of the adhesion of the coating to the substrate, but also makes it easier to compare the adhesion of several coatings to each other [CITE:ev_03d5cf9642fafd22].\n\nTesting speed is another variable influencing peel test outcomes. Peel tests measure adhesive strength by pulling one material away from another at a specific angle and speed [CITE:ev_f0f1594bc5d9dd20]. In one study, peel speed was varied from 0.5 to 1.5mm/s to test effects on three coatings [CITE:ev_bd20a3afb1754c5c]. Results showed little dependence on peel speed, but low adhesion strength of silicones was apparent [CITE:ev_1377196d9ff7c638]. This shows that while speed is a controlled parameter in standards, it may not be the dominant factor for certain polymer chemistries like silicone. For silicone materials specifically, comprehensive adhesive suitability requires comparing peel adhesion, tensile strength, and shear resistance [CITE:ev_ac671c6a322fe8bd]. Peel force is applied to an edge of the adherend and substrate and determined from average value [CITE:ev_ab6e9530d9b2562d].\n\nMaterial suitability varies significantly across testing methods. Crosshatch testing is most suitable for thin coatings on rigid substrates [CITE:ev_3550bf7b3e0ae5a8]. The cross-cut test is especially suitable for thin layers on rigid substrates like metal or plastic [CITE:ev_bb2ddf4927283db1]. The cross-cut test method is limited by coating thickness with maximum of 250 micrometers [CITE:ev_a256fbf9c6062207]. Similarly, evidence indicates the cross-cut test is applicable for layer thicknesses up to about 250 \u00b5m [CITE:ev_df003953c7f7a2d0]. In contrast, peel testing is suitable for designs where bonded edges experience prying or bending [CITE:ev_1be768df70c46a07]. This makes peel testing more appropriate for flexible polymer coatings such as urethanes and silicones used in roofing or conformal applications, whereas cross-cut methods favor rigid epoxy systems on metal. Cross hatch tape testing is intended for laboratory use on coatings under 5 mils thickness [CITE:ev_c8b75c8d91e7faea].\n\nStandards compliance ensures reproducibility across these variations. ASTM D1876 and D6862 standardize T-peel and 90-degree peel tests respectively [CITE:ev_16c2d5e21aa178ca]. ISO 8510-2 covers 180-degree peel test for flexible-bonded-to-rigid test specimen assembly [CITE:ev_75d316de9669a24a]. For cross-cut methods, ISO 2409 specifies the cross-cut test method for evaluating adhesion of coatings on sheet metal parts [CITE:ev_067cc0abd42529d2]. Additionally, JIS K 5600-5-6: 1999 specifies the Cross-Cut Test method for coating adhesion evaluation under Japanese Industrial Standards [CITE:ev_38e4b21684c9f29c]. Pull-off testing follows ISO 4624, ASTM D4541, and D7234 standards for evaluating coating peel strength [CITE:ev_0ffc84130522e5ce]. ASTM D4541 and BS EN ISO 4624 describe pull-off adhesion testing standards for protective coatings [CITE:ev_befc6654b3c8f5ad]. The existence of multiple standards for similar measurements indicates that regulatory compliance depends on the specific industry sector, such as automotive versus construction. Chinese national standard GB/T 2790-1995 specifies the 180\u00b0 peel strength test method steps and requirements [CITE:ev_4ec63e8773cfcd3e]. This global proliferation of standards necessitates careful selection based on geographic regulatory requirements. ISO 4624 is referenced as a global standard for pull-off adhesion testing compliance [CITE:ev_a9eaff2972823a42].\n\nSample preparation introduces further variables affecting data integrity. Peel test uses 1 inch wide by 6 inch long fabric strip embedded in coating base layer [CITE:ev_14c32495c2f3b455]. However, conflicting evidence suggests a coating adhesion test requires pulling cheesecloth at 180 degree angle at slow steady rate without specifying dimensions [CITE:ev_13478b581fb5d9cc]. Standard testing procedures evaluate peel force under constant temperature 23\u00b11\u00b0C and 50\u00b15% RH [CITE:ev_4420310a1914e20]. Environmental control is therefore critical when comparing data across different laboratories or field conditions. Roof coating must cure minimum 72 hours before conducting peel adhesion test [CITE:ev_9cc92ec315452ccc]. This curing requirement is critical for urethane and silicone roof coatings where full chemical crosslinking takes time. Pull-off adhesion testing measures adhesive strength of coatings after application and curing per guidelines [CITE:ev_2b7ca35715831266].\n\nPerformance scoring differs between quantitative and qualitative methods. Cross-cut adhesion test evaluation ranges from 0B representing complete detachment to 5B representing no detachment [CITE:ev_d0d69c4b450fa1e3]. Method A results assessed on 0A-5A scale where 5A indicates no peeling and 0A indicates complete removal [CITE:ev_5311f47cd26b20c1]. Tape test procedures involve applying pressure-sensitive adhesive tape on cross-hatched coating area and removing quickly [CITE:ev_2669ad91a2f8accd]. The tape test is qualitative and cannot precisely assess quantitatively the actual strength of the adhesion [CITE:ev_389d441cc3fe3ba4]. Independent laboratories have noted differences of up to 50 percent in tape adhesion variability over time [CITE:ev_ce3124033536bdb1]. This high variability supports the use of peel tests for critical applications requiring precise numerical data. However, paint and tape adhesion tests are useful for screening surface prep and cure on coatings [CITE:ev_be43a72512267deb]. Thus, tape methods serve a quality control function while peel and pull-off methods serve performance validation functions. ASTM D3359 is the standard method for rating adhesion by tape test [CITE:ev_d2c3f9dace34682e].\n\nMaterial specific performance also dictates method selection. Adhesive formulations are designed to provide excellent adhesion and high shear and peel strength [CITE:ev_8711bd68ae7dca95]. Results showed good agreement between hot water-adhesion tests and TxDOT peel tests [CITE:ev_6c07ce38730c09f8]. For urethane and epoxy comparisons, adhesive suitability requires comparing peel adhesion, tensile strength, and shear resistance [CITE:ev_ac671c6a322fe8bd]. Silicone materials exhibited low adhesion strength in peel contexts [CITE:ev_1377196d9ff7c638]. This aligns with the known lower surface energy of silicones compared to epoxies and urethanes. Consequently, testing silicone coatings may require more sensitive peel configurations or surface energy measurements as complementary methods [CITE:ev_d77bb9a950ab7132]. Epoxy coatings must conform to MPI ASM No. 116 with polyamide-cured epoxy-resin material for finish coats [CITE:ev_b1b93517b8c30bf6].\n\nThickness constraints further limit method applicability. Cross-cut testing measures coating resistance to separation by cutting a right-angle lattice pattern to the substrate [CITE:ev_0c29c65200b8e0c0]. However, test method is commonly used for coating thicknesses in the range of 0.1 to 30 micrometers [CITE:ev_8858c4a9dba538b2]. This narrow range excludes many thick polymer linings used in industrial settings. Pull-off testing measures adhesive strength of coatings after application and curing per guidelines [CITE:ev_2b7ca35715831266]. Valid pull-off test requires coating to cover at least 50% of stamp surface after detachment [CITE:ev_d0994f38fa60bebe]. This failure mode requirement ensures that cohesive failure within the coating is distinguished from adhesive failure at the interface. Test method covers determination of practical adhesion strength of hard ceramic coatings on metal and ceramic substrates [CITE:ev_f6c7a8ecd3f37045].\n\nRegulatory frameworks reinforce these methodological distinctions. ASTM D7234 defines the standard test method for pull-off adhesion strength on concrete using portable testers [CITE:ev_3a7d481c74614432]. The T shaped peel test ASTM D1876 is most widely used for thin-gauge metal adherends [CITE:ev_e3a5ffd440e5e83a]. Four common methods exist for testing adhesion of protective coatings and linings per ASTM standards [CITE:ev_c53e1e15e6c509b0]. Common adhesion measuring techniques are performed using a knife or a pull-off adhesion tester [CITE:ev_ac710f7b1e8e52c6]. Test assesses adhesion of film coatings to metallic substrates using pressure-sensitive tape [CITE:ev_fbfc14691b574d36]. ATS offers ASTM paint adhesion testing to determine adhesive performance according to industry standards [CITE:ev_22e6e69ce94d4f2b].\n\nPeel force peaks occur at 60\u00b0 and 180\u00b0 peel angles where more peeling force is borne by tape backing [CITE:ev_a9a22d0a16279bcb]. This mechanical phenomenon shows that 90-degree configurations might offer a more direct measure of interfacial adhesion by minimizing backing deformation effects. Test Method A (X-Cut Tape Test) is designed primarily for field use with X-shaped cut in coating [CITE:ev_e46b8904dd9b8226]. Test Method B (Cross-Cut Tape Test) is preferred for laboratory or controlled environments with lattice pattern [CITE:ev_a673f1e5d40af5bb]. This division between field and laboratory methods underscores the trade-off between precision and portability. Cross-cut tape peel test was used to evaluate the adhesion property of polymeric coatings by cutting a lattice pattern of small squares [CITE:ev_1962a4977f7b146f].\n\nSurface contaminants and preparation impact all methods. Surface free energy and adhesion parameter measurements proposed as complementary method to traditional cross-cut tape tests [CITE:ev_d77bb9a950ab7132]. Using a calibrated knife reduced subjectivity found in other adhesion tests [CITE:ev_5caae816b34f9fb7]. Cross-Cut Test creates lattice pattern through coating without cutting into the substrate [CITE:ev_75c27b2cdb14b23e]. However, Cross-cut tape peel test was used to evaluate the adhesion property of polymeric coatings by cutting a lattice pattern of small squares [CITE:ev_1962a4977f7b146f]. Consistency in cut depth is therefore paramount for reproducible cross-cut results. Cross-Cut Test procedure involves making lattice-pattern cuts through coating to substrate, applying adhesive tape, and peeling in one motion [CITE:ev_4add28fe26ddd5e3]. Scrape Test procedure applies adhesive to smooth substrate at uniform thickness then uses rounded stylus [CITE:ev_16d5eda9ab029ec2].\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n*   Peel test configurations (90-degree, 180-degree, T-peel) provide quantitative force per width data (N/mm or lb/in) suitable for flexible polymer coatings, unlike the qualitative 0B-5B classification of cross-cut tests [CITE:ev_44fea46094d83d2a][CITE:ev_d0d69c4b450fa1e3][CITE:ev_16c2d5e21aa178ca].\n*   Cross-cut testing is restricted to thin coatings (maximum 250 micrometers) on rigid substrates, whereas peel and pull-off methods accommodate broader thickness ranges and flexible materials [CITE:ev_a256fbf9c6062207][CITE:ev_3550bf7b3e0ae5a8][CITE:ev_1be768df70c46a07].\n*   Peel speed variations (0.5 to 1.5mm/s) showed little dependence in silicone materials, suggesting chemistry may influence speed sensitivity more than test geometry [CITE:ev_1377196d9ff7c638][CITE:ev_bd20a3afb1754c5c][CITE:ev_ac671c6a322fe8bd].\n*   Methodological conflicts exist regarding whether tests measure fundamental adhesion strength or practical bond resistance, with evidence favoring the latter for engineering applications [CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d][CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00][CITE:ev_84a08742318ed15d].\n*   Regulatory compliance requires adherence to specific standards such as ASTM D1876 for T-peel or ISO 2409 for cross-cut, depending on substrate type and geographic jurisdiction [CITE:ev_e3a5ffd440e5e83a][CITE:ev_067cc0abd42529d2][CITE:ev_4ec63e8773cfcd3e].\n\n## Sample Preparation and Thickness Control for Peel Tests\n\nSample preparation protocols establish the foundational validity of adhesion data across polymer coating systems. Variations in coating application and curing protocols introduce significant variability when comparing epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials across pull-off, cross-cut, and peel test methods [CITE:ev_315057e2cc7dd3f8]. Preparation procedures dictate whether a test yields quantitative tensile strength or qualitative classification, directly impacting the comparative analysis of polymer coating performance [CITE:ev_84a08742318ed15d]. For peel tests, the physical configuration of the specimen is critical to obtaining reproducible force measurements [CITE:ev_27f9ac91a5f22e9b]. Evidence indicates that peel adhesion samples were applied to standard micro-finish stainless steel panels using a roller to ensure uniformity [CITE:ev_51f5bdc9c905183d]. The geometry of the strip influences the stress distribution during testing [CITE:ev_1ae4530e37be2c91]. One protocol specifies that peel tests use a 1 inch wide by 6 inch long fabric strip embedded in the coating base layer [CITE:ev_14c32495c2f3b455]. Conversely, alternative specifications for silicone coating roofing adhesion tests utilize a cheesecloth strip 1 inch by 12 inches embedded in the wet coating [CITE:ev_234e921351755d24]. This discrepancy in strip dimensions represents a significant methodological conflict that affects stress concentration and total force required for separation [CITE:ev_aabe5f967b113f45]. The variation suggests that direct comparison of peel strength values between studies using different strip lengths requires normalization based on film width and peel length [CITE:ev_1ae4530e37be2c91].\n\nThickness control serves as a primary constraint determining the suitability of specific test methods for different polymer coatings. Cross-cut tests are generally restricted to thinner coatings, whereas pull-off methods accommodate greater thicknesses [CITE:ev_df003953c7f7a2d0]. The cross-cut test method is limited by coating thickness with a maximum of 250 micrometers [CITE:ev_a256fbf9c6062207]. Similarly, other standards indicate the cross-cut test is applicable for layer thicknesses up to about 250 \u00b5m [CITE:ev_df003953c7f7a2d0]. In contrast, specific test methods are commonly used for coating thicknesses in the range of 0.1 to 30 micrometers [CITE:ev_8858c4a9dba538b2]. Laboratory environments often prefer cross hatch tape testing for coatings under 5 mils thickness [CITE:ev_c8b75c8d91e7faea]. These constraints imply that thick epoxy coatings exceed the limits of cross-cut evaluation, necessitating pull-off methods for accurate assessment [CITE:ev_bb2ddf4927283db1].\n\n| Test Method Constraint | Thickness Limit | Unit | Evidence Source |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| Cross-Cut Test Maximum | 250 | micrometers | [CITE:ev_a256fbf9c6062207] |\n| Cross-Cut Test Applicable | 250 | \u00b5m | [CITE:ev_df003953c7f7a2d0] |\n| General Test Method Range | 0.1 to 30 | micrometers | [CITE:ev_8858c4a9dba538b2] |\n| Cross Hatch Laboratory Use | Under 5 | mils | [CITE:ev_c8b75c8d91e7faea] |\n\nTable 1 illustrates the overlapping yet distinct thickness ranges for adhesion evaluation. The variation in units (micrometers vs. mils) across standards complicates direct regulatory compliance comparisons without conversion, though the evidence preserves original units [CITE:ev_c8b75c8d91e7faea]. Tape test results are dependent on paint thickness, affecting comparability between different coating systems [CITE:ev_6666c9bcf54e3c33]. This dependency is particularly relevant when comparing silicone materials, which often utilize thinner conformal layers, against structural epoxy coatings [CITE:ev_1377196d9ff7c638]. Consequently, thickness control is not merely a procedural step but a determinant of method validity [CITE:ev_3550bf7b3e0ae5a8].\n\nCuring conditions prior to testing significantly influence adhesion performance metrics. Roof coating must cure a minimum of 72 hours before conducting a peel adhesion test [CITE:ev_9cc92ec315452ccc]. Premature testing risks measuring cohesive strength of the uncured polymer rather than adhesive bond strength to the substrate [CITE:ev_2b7ca35715831266]. Pull-off adhesion testing measures adhesive strength of coatings after application and curing per guidelines [CITE:ev_2b7ca35715831266]. The quality of coating application varies by coater and impacts adhesion results [CITE:ev_315057e2cc7dd3f8]. Standard testing procedures evaluate peel force under constant conditions to minimize environmental variance [CITE:ev_be43a72512267deb]. These environmental controls are essential when comparing temperature-sensitive silicone materials against more rigid epoxy systems [CITE:ev_1377196d9ff7c638].\n\nConflicting evidence regarding delamination rates highlights the sensitivity of preparation variables. One study reported that PUA coating without silane treatment had 6.75% delamination in a tape peel test [CITE:ev_df0f1276d85b68da]. In direct contradiction, another source states that PUA+4SR coating without silane treatment showed approximately 95% delamination (94.93%) [CITE:ev_379a35c45a4fa40f]. This vast divergence suggests that minor variations in nanoparticle filling or substrate preparation drastically alter failure modes [CITE:ev_379a35c45a4fa40f]. The position supporting higher delamination rates aligns with evidence that low adhesion strength of silicones was apparent in results showing little dependence on peel speed [CITE:ev_1377196d9ff7c638]. Therefore, the 95% delamination figure likely reflects the inherent difficulty in bonding silicone-modified systems without coupling agents, whereas the 6.75% figure represents a specific formulation variant [CITE:ev_df0f1276d85b68da].\n\nFurther conflict exists regarding the fundamental capability of testing methods to measure adhesion. One source states the pull-off adhesion test measures the strength of the bond between a coating and the surface [CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00]. However, contradictory evidence asserts that the test method does not measure fundamental adhesion strength of the bond between coating and substrate [CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d]. This disagreement stems from the distinction between practical adhesion and thermodynamic adhesion [CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d]. Pull-off testing measures coating resistance to separation using perpendicular tensile force, which includes cohesive failure components [CITE:ev_f99cba7616963c64]. Cross-cut testing measures coating resistance to separation by cutting a right-angle lattice pattern to the substrate [CITE:ev_0c29c65200b8e0c0]. The Cross-Cut Test provides qualitative evaluation while the Pull-Off Method provides quantitative evaluation of coating adhesion [CITE:ev_84a08742318ed15d]. Thus, while pull-off tests yield numerical data, they do not necessarily isolate the interfacial bond strength from the bulk material strength [CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00].\n\nPreparation for cross-cut tests involves specific geometric constraints to ensure valid classification. The cross-cut test uses a tool to cut a right-angle lattice pattern through the coating to the substrate [CITE:ev_183b421be46e5e8b]. This lattice pattern cut into the coating down to the substrate facilitates adhesive tape removal [CITE:ev_3fadc73633d06ff8]. The Cross-Cut Test classifies adhesion results into six levels according to the degree of peeling after tape removal [CITE:ev_cd6ffc422440dd18]. Results are assessed on a 0A-5A scale where 5A indicates no peeling and 0A indicates complete removal [CITE:ev_5311f47cd26b20c1]. Cross-cut adhesion test evaluation ranges from 0B representing complete detachment to 5B representing no detachment [CITE:ev_d0d69c4b450fa1e3]. The variation in scaling (0A-5A vs 0B-5B) across evidence sources indicates potential inconsistencies in standard adoption between ASTM and ISO frameworks [CITE:ev_815bc543dbac8f04]. ASTM D3359 outlines standardized test methods for evaluating adhesion of coatings to metallic substrates using pressure-sensitive tape [CITE:ev_815bc543dbac8f04].\n\nValid pull-off test execution requires specific coverage criteria to ensure data integrity. A valid pull-off test requires coating to cover at least 50% of stamp surface after detachment [CITE:ev_d0994f38fa60bebe]. If the failure occurs within the adhesive used to attach the dolly rather than the coating itself, the data is invalid [CITE:ev_d0994f38fa60bebe]. The study objective was to identify an optimal adhesive for the pull-off test providing higher adhesion [CITE:ev_f8a2b17c15a40670]. This underscores that the testing adhesive itself is a variable in sample preparation [CITE:ev_f8a2b17c15a40670]. Pull-off force thickness dependence agreed with modified Kendall model for elastomeric coatings, linking physical preparation to theoretical modeling [CITE:ev_19047670aed8064b].\n\nSurface preparation extends beyond coating application to include substrate conditioning. Site adhesion testing must be verified according to criteria to confirm proper surface preparation [CITE:ev_97840fd9469c02ca]. Paint and tape adhesion tests are useful for screening surface prep and cure on coatings [CITE:ev_be43a72512267deb]. Surface free energy and adhesion parameter measurements are proposed as complementary methods to traditional cross-cut tape tests [CITE:ev_d77bb9a950ab7132]. This suggests that physical cutting methods may not fully capture the chemical readiness of the substrate, particularly for low-energy surfaces like silicone [CITE:ev_d77bb9a950ab7132]. Adhesion test must be conducted prior to estimating coating restoration project per Inland adhesion testing procedures [CITE:ev_61c41c390dad6a70].\n\nThe implications of these preparation variables are critical for the comparative analysis of epoxy, urethane, and silicone. Epoxy coatings, often thicker, align well with pull-off protocols but exceed cross-cut thickness limits [CITE:ev_a256fbf9c6062207]. Silicone materials, frequently applied as thin conformal coatings, fit within cross-cut parameters but exhibit low adhesion strength in peel tests [CITE:ev_1377196d9ff7c638]. Peel and adhesion strength testing measures bond strength when force attempts separation [CITE:ev_27f9ac91a5f22e9b]. Peel testing is suitable for designs where bonded edges experience prying or bending [CITE:ev_1be768df70c46a07].\n\nMeasurement units and reporting standards further complicate cross-method comparison. Peel strength is calculated using force applied during peeling process divided by film width [CITE:ev_1ae4530e37be2c91]. Scrape test adhesion is measured in kilograms of force required for removal [CITE:ev_73f6c6e661c92e30]. Pull-off method provides values for peel stress allowing quick determination and comparison of coating adhesion [CITE:ev_03d5cf9642fafd22]. However, peel-back and pull-off data should not be compared since testing mechanisms differ [CITE:ev_aabe5f967b113f45]. This prohibition is vital for the research question; combining pull-off MPa data with peel N/mm data without mechanistic context yields erroneous conclusions about material superiority [CITE:ev_aabe5f967b113f45].\n\nIndependent laboratories have noted differences of up to 50 percent in tape adhesion variability over time [CITE:ev_ce3124033536bdb1]. This variability emphasizes the need for strict thickness and curing controls when benchmarking polymer coatings [CITE:ev_ce3124033536bdb1]. Crosshatch testing is most suitable for thin coatings on rigid substrates [CITE:ev_3550bf7b3e0ae5a8]. The cross-cut test is especially suitable for thin layers on rigid substrates like metal or plastic [CITE:ev_bb2ddf4927283db1]. Four common methods exist for testing adhesion of protective coatings and linings per ASTM standards [CITE:ev_c53e1e15e6c509b0]. Tape and pull-off tests are common methods for evaluating coating adhesion to substrates [CITE:ev_fee4deed784b02c8].\n\nThe selection of preparation method dictates the failure mode observed. Adhesion is rated by inspecting the cut area for coating removal relative to classifications [CITE:ev_d23f5ac9162865a3]. Mode of failure and pull-off pressure confirm proper surface preparation and coating application [CITE:ev_399c2dba04cb99b8]. Test method covers determination of practical adhesion strength of hard ceramic coatings on metal and ceramic substrates [CITE:ev_f6c7a8ecd3f37045]. Using a calibrated knife reduced subjectivity found in other adhesion tests [CITE:ev_5caae816b34f9fb7]. This reduction in subjectivity is crucial for regulatory compliance where binary pass/fail criteria are enforced [CITE:ev_5caae816b34f9fb7].\n\nIn the context of silicone roofing, specific preparation mandates exist. Peel adhesion test kit measures force required to pull coating off primed surface [CITE:ev_092f7ee5d2103ef4]. Hot water and knife adhesion tests were validated as valuable tools for quality control in this study [CITE:ev_cca3f9b996f96bd9]. Hot water and knife tests effectively discriminated between good and bad quality coatings [CITE:ev_6d68babb48b5d010]. Results showed good agreement between hot water-adhesion tests and TxDOT peel tests [CITE:ev_6c07ce38730c09f8]. These alternative preparation methods offer robustness against environmental variables that standard peel tests might not capture [CITE:ev_6c07ce38730c09f8].\n\nUltimately, sample preparation acts as the filter through which material performance is viewed. Inconsistent thickness control invalidates comparisons between epoxy and silicone systems due to their differing mechanical responses to stress [CITE:ev_19047670aed8064b]. The evidence supports a structured approach where thickness limits define the method, and curing times define the validity [CITE:ev_9cc92ec315452ccc]. Adhesive formulations are designed to provide excellent adhesion and high shear and peel strength [CITE:ev_8711bd68ae7dca95]. Comprehensive adhesive suitability requires comparing peel adhesion, tensile strength, and shear resistance [CITE:ev_ac671c6a322fe8bd]. By adhering to strict preparation protocols, the comparative analysis of polymer coatings can isolate material properties from procedural artifacts [CITE:ev_ac671c6a322fe8bd].\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n*   Cross-cut test methods are limited to coating thicknesses up to 250 micrometers, restricting their use for thick epoxy systems compared to pull-off methods [CITE:ev_a256fbf9c6062207].\n*   Conflicting evidence regarding delamination rates (6.75% vs 95% for PUA without silane) indicates high sensitivity to nanoparticle filling and substrate preparation variables [CITE:ev_df0f1276d85b68da].\n*   Peel-back and pull-off data cannot be directly compared due to differing testing mechanisms, necessitating separate evaluation tracks for epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials [CITE:ev_aabe5f967b113f45].\n*   Valid pull-off testing requires at least 50% coating coverage on the stamp surface after detachment to ensure measurement of coating adhesion rather than adhesive failure [CITE:ev_d0994f38fa60bebe].\n*   Standard testing procedures mandate constant environmental conditions to minimize variance in peel force measurements [CITE:ev_be43a72512267deb].\n\n![Measurement Parameters](data:image/png;base64,)\n*Figure 1: Bar chart showing numeric values of measurement parameters like hardness and thickness.*\n\n\n\n![Standard Timeline](data:image/png;base64,)\n*Figure 2: Timeline showing adoption, reapproval, and publication years.*\n\n\n\n| Parameter | Value | Unit |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| Document Page Count | 29 | pages |\n| Vickers Hardness Threshold | 5 | GPa |\n| Coating Thickness Limit | 30 | \u00b5m |\n| Diamond Indenter Included Angle | 120 | degrees |\n| Diamond Indenter Tip Radius | 200 | \u00b5m |\n| Applicable Coating Thickness Minimum | 0.1 | \u00b5m |\n| Applicable Coating Thickness Maximum | 30 | \u00b5m |\n\n*Table 1: Summary of Technical Measurements*\n\n\n## Surface Contaminants and Adhesive Failure Types\n\nSurface contaminants represent a primary variable affecting adhesion test outcomes, as oils, dust, or moisture form a barrier between adhesive and surface weakening bond [CITE:ev_4ef566dfab383143]. The presence of such contaminants directly impacts the measured adhesion strength, with solvent wiping of contaminated surfaces increasing bond strength from 0.8 MPa to 10.9 MPa for Epoxy D [CITE:ev_a3334666bd32dd8a]. This thirteen-fold improvement demonstrates that contamination control is not merely a preparatory step but a determinant of whether testing methods accurately reflect coating performance. Surface energy mismatch prevents proper wetting when substrate energy is lower than adhesive surface energy, creating conditions where adhesion tests indicate failure despite adequate coating formulation [CITE:ev_ea1b9ed2aa2e3a82]. High surface energy materials such as metals and glass promote strong adhesive bonds, whereas low-energy substrates require additional surface treatment to achieve comparable adhesion metrics [CITE:ev_fbc506f470d4da9e]. Untreated PA showed lowest work of adhesion at 69.7 mN/m with highest interfacial tension leading to weak coating retention, quantifying the impact of surface treatment on adhesion parameters [CITE:ev_96b127d3014b3201].\n\nAdhesion strength measurements vary significantly across material types and surface conditions, establishing a performance envelope for structural applications. The following table compares quantitative adhesion values derived from distinct testing contexts and material systems.\n\n| Material/Context | Adhesion Strength | Condition/Standard |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| Epoxy D | 0.8 MPa | Contaminated Surface [CITE:ev_a3334666bd32dd8a] |\n| Epoxy D | 10.9 MPa | Solvent Wiped [CITE:ev_a3334666bd32dd8a] |\n| Hydroxyapatite | 3.4 \u00b1 1.0 MPa | ASTM Test Methods [CITE:ev_5bd33c7a02d9cd28] |\n| Industrial Adhesives | 10 MPa to over 60 MPa | Structural Applications [CITE:ev_a85f361a2bdff124] |\n\nTesting methodologies respond differently to contamination levels and substrate conditions. Pull-off adhesion test measures the strength of the bond between a coating and the surface it is applied to, providing quantitative values for peel stress allowing quick determination and comparison of coating adhesion [CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00][CITE:ev_03d5cf9642fafd22]. The force measured in MPa or psi reveals the coating's tensile pull-off strength indicating adhesion [CITE:ev_11ae17d7fc3f76c6]. However, conflicting evidence suggests this test method does not measure fundamental adhesion strength of the bond between coating and substrate, indicating limitations in what pull-off values actually represent [CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d]. This contradiction requires resolution: while pull-off testing provides numerical strength values in MPa or psi revealing the coating's tensile pull-off strength indicating adhesion, these values reflect practical adhesion rather than intrinsic molecular bonding [CITE:ev_11ae17d7fc3f76c6]. ASTM D4541 and BS EN ISO 4624 describe pull-off adhesion testing standards for protective coatings, establishing international consistency for quantitative assessment [CITE:ev_befc6654b3c8f5ad]. ISO 4624 is referenced as a global standard for pull-off adhesion testing compliance, further validating the method for regulatory contexts [CITE:ev_a9eaff2972823a42].\n\nCross-cut testing offers qualitative evaluation contrasting with pull-off quantitative assessment [CITE:ev_84a08742318ed15d]. ASTM D3359 outlines standardized test methods for evaluating adhesion of coatings to metallic substrates using pressure-sensitive tape, with the standard codified in 1974 [CITE:ev_815bc543dbac8f04][CITE:ev_cee97c994fefc9b1]. The cross-cut test classifies adhesion results into six levels according to the degree of peeling after tape removal, providing categorical rather than continuous data [CITE:ev_cd6ffc422440dd18]. ISO 2409 specifies the cross-cut test method for evaluating adhesion of coatings on sheet metal parts, establishing international consistency for this qualitative approach [CITE:ev_067cc0abd42529d2]. JIS K 5600-5-6: 1999 specifies the Cross-Cut Test method for coating adhesion evaluation under Japanese Industrial Standards, further demonstrating global standardization of this method [CITE:ev_38e4b21684c9f29c]. The tape test is qualitative and cannot precisely assess quantitatively the actual strength of the adhesion, limiting its utility for comparing epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials on a continuous scale [CITE:ev_389d441cc3fe3ba4]. Cross hatch tape testing is intended for laboratory use on coatings under 5 mils thickness, restricting field applicability for thicker polymer systems [CITE:ev_c8b75c8d91e7faea].\n\nPeel tests measure adhesive strength by pulling one material away from another at a specific angle and speed, offering a third methodological approach [CITE:ev_f0f1594bc5d9dd20]. Comprehensive adhesive suitability requires comparing peel adhesion, tensile strength, and shear resistance, suggesting no single test method suffices for complete material characterization [CITE:ev_ac671c6a322fe8bd]. Results showed good agreement between hot water-adhesion tests and TxDOT peel tests, indicating peel methods correlate with environmental exposure performance [CITE:ev_6c07ce38730c09f8]. For silicone materials specifically, cure improves adhesion except for silicone, where failure occurred at the tape interface, suggesting peel testing underestimates silicone coating performance [CITE:ev_0db5383239a26b95]. Traditional adhesion tests like ISO/JIS cross-hatch and ASTM tape tests only evaluate final adhesion strength without explaining adhesion mechanisms, limiting diagnostic capability for failure analysis [CITE:ev_60e99e00ac021116]. Surface free energy and adhesion parameter measurements proposed as complementary method to traditional cross-cut tape tests, suggesting contamination assessment requires both traditional testing and surface science approaches [CITE:ev_d77bb9a950ab7132].\n\nFailure mode classification distinguishes between adhesive and cohesive failure, with critical implications for interpreting test results. Adhesive failure indicates molecular attraction between adhesive and substrate was weaker than internal adhesive strength [CITE:ev_1826db32940b1bde]. Adhesive failure occurs when adhesive peels away cleanly from substrate surface indicating poor surface preparation or contamination [CITE:ev_a1b14fef9fc9b89b]. Conversely, cohesive strength decreases as coating undergoes weathering and pull-off strength monitors breakdown, meaning cohesive failure within the coating layer indicates material degradation rather than bond failure [CITE:ev_8001a26420bf8b10]. Acceptable adhesion shows cohesive tear within sealant or elongation beyond movement capability, establishing that cohesive failure within the adhesive layer represents successful bonding [CITE:ev_52375915486842b0]. Glue failure involves visible separation of the glue from itself, the coating, or dolly, providing visual indicators for failure classification [CITE:ev_b53f0a2c5edbc39f]. Mechanical adhesion occurs when adhesive penetrates microscopic ridges and valleys of substrate anchoring the coating, distinguishing mechanical interlocking from chemical bonding mechanisms [CITE:ev_11cf92f65a363078].\n\nMaterial-specific performance varies significantly across epoxy, urethane, and silicone coatings. Nine common adhesives including cyanoacrylic and epoxy were selected and tested on specimens, providing comparative data across adhesive chemistries [CITE:ev_3f84be5fe88e4623]. Most cyanoacrylic adhesives failed with breakage occurring in the adhesive film during testing, indicating cohesive failure modes dominate for this material class [CITE:ev_90cd35013a72b096]. Two different epoxy adhesives were characterized including high elongation high toughness epoxy and toughened epoxy adhesive, demonstrating formulation variations affect failure modes [CITE:ev_61cc13e36041ee9a]. Adhesive formulations are designed to provide excellent adhesion and high shear and peel strength, with performance varying by polymer chemistry [CITE:ev_8711bd68ae7dca95]. Adhesion variance results from surface treatment or the nature of the substrate, emphasizing that test results reflect both coating and substrate conditions [CITE:ev_eb6a7ba09f91b0a1]. Coating application quality varies by coater and impacts adhesion, introducing human factors into test variability [CITE:ev_315057e2cc7dd3f8].\n\nField testing considerations differ from laboratory protocols. Field adhesion tests must be performed at the shop or jobsite to verify sealant adhesion, establishing location requirements for compliance [CITE:ev_5da5d464178b2eea]. Field adhesion test also called hand pull test verifies proper sealant installation, providing rapid on-site assessment capability [CITE:ev_7658ca702fb6e4fa]. Field adhesion test recommended when laboratory test cannot be performed on-site, acknowledging practical constraints in coating application environments [CITE:ev_f569492a9905b1d9]. Adhesion test must be conducted prior to estimating coating restoration project per Inland adhesion testing procedures, establishing timing requirements for assessment [CITE:ev_61c41c390dad6a70]. Paint and tape adhesion tests are useful for screening surface prep and cure on coatings, indicating these methods serve quality control functions rather than definitive strength measurement [CITE:ev_be43a72512267deb]. Independent laboratories have noted differences of up to 50 percent in tape adhesion variability over time, quantifying inter-laboratory variation that affects result comparability [CITE:ev_ce3124033536bdb1].\n\nAdhesion strength of coating to substrate defines overall coating integrity and performance, establishing the stakes for accurate testing [CITE:ev_2852c4416e3e0b87]. Evaluating adhesion before and after exposure lengths helps estimate adhesion retention, recommending longitudinal testing approaches [CITE:ev_02597b3988622977]. Mode of failure and pull-off pressure confirm proper surface preparation and coating application, linking test outcomes to process verification [CITE:ev_399c2dba04cb99b8]. Failure to verify and document adhesion results may result in adhesion loss not covered by warranty, establishing documentation requirements for liability protection [CITE:ev_a889a20cea5550f0]. All structural applications must be tested by the Dow test lab for surface preparation recommendations, indicating manufacturer-specific protocols for critical applications [CITE:ev_24d4477c6b68a302]. Adhesive bonds can be stronger than the materials being bonded together in some automotive applications, demonstrating that cohesive substrate failure represent optimal bonding performance [CITE:ev_20783c49fa127283]. Valid pull-off test requires coating to cover at least 50% of stamp surface after detachment, establishing validity criteria for quantitative data [CITE:ev_d0994f38fa60bebe]. Test method covers determination of practical adhesion strength of hard ceramic coatings on metal and ceramic substrates, extending applicability beyond polymer systems [CITE:ev_f6c7a8ecd3f37045]. Test method is commonly used for coating thicknesses in the range of 0.1 to 30 micrometers, defining dimensional constraints for accurate assessment [CITE:ev_8858c4a9dba538b2].\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n*   Surface contaminants such as oils, dust, or moisture form barriers between adhesive and surface, with solvent wiping increasing epoxy bond strength from 0.8 MPa to 10.9 MPa, demonstrating contamination control directly determines test outcomes [CITE:ev_4ef566dfab383143][CITE:ev_a3334666bd32dd8a].\n*   Pull-off testing provides quantitative values in MPa or psi for coating adhesion comparison, though evidence conflicts on whether it measures fundamental bond strength versus practical adhesion strength [CITE:ev_11ae17d7fc3f76c6][CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00][CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d].\n*   Cross-cut testing per ASTM D3359 (codified 1974) and ISO 2409 provides qualitative six-level classification, contrasting with pull-off quantitative assessment and limiting material comparison precision [CITE:ev_cee97c994fefc9b1][CITE:ev_067cc0abd42529d2][CITE:ev_cd6ffc422440dd18].\n*   Adhesive failure indicates weaker molecular attraction between adhesive and substrate than internal adhesive strength, whereas cohesive failure within the adhesive layer represents acceptable adhesion performance [CITE:ev_1826db32940b1bde][CITE:ev_52375915486842b0].\n*   Independent laboratories report up to 50 percent variability in tape adhesion testing, requiring multiple test methods and contamination controls for reliable epoxy, urethane, and silicone material comparison [CITE:ev_ce3124033536bdb1][CITE:ev_ac671c6a322fe8bd].\n\n![Measurement Specifications](data:image/png;base64,[31;1mInvalidOperation: [31;1mCannot set property. Property setting is supported only on core types in this language mode.[0m

  520: Clinicians are strongly advised to monitor renal function meticulously in patients 
  521: reporting persistent, severe gastrointestinal symptoms to prevent acute volume 
  522: depletion.34 
  523: Hypoglycemia Management 
  524: As a dual incretin agonist, tirzepatide stimulates insulin secretion in a strictly 
  525: glucose-dependent manner; the mechanism ceases when blood glucose levels return to 
  526: normal. Therefore, as a monotherapy, the intrinsic risk of clinically significant hypoglycemia is 
  527: exceptionally low.10 However, the risk escalates significantly when tirzepatide is 
  528: co-administered with medications that cause glucose-independent insulin release, primarily 
  529: insulin secretagogues (e.g., sulfonylureas like glimepiride or glipizide) or exogenous insulin.34 
  530: Both FDA and Health Canada guidelines mandate that clinicians must proactively consider 
  531: significant dose reductions for concomitant insulin or sulfonylureas upon the initiation of 
  532: tirzepatide to prevent severe, potentially life-threatening hypoglycemic episodes.34 
  533: Boxed Warning: Thyroid C-Cell Tumors 
  534: The most severe regulatory caution regarding tirzepatide is its Boxed Warning (FDA) and 
  535: Serious Warnings and Precautions box (Health Canada) regarding the potential risk of thyroid 
  536: C-cell tumors.34 In extensive rodent toxicology studies, tirzepatide caused dose-dependent 
  537: and treatment-duration-dependent thyroid C-cell tumors, including both benign adenomas 
  538: and malignant carcinomas, at clinically relevant systemic exposures.35 
  539: The human relevance of this rodent-specific mechanism—which is believed to be mediated via 
  540: sustained GLP-1 receptor activation on rodent C-cells—remains undetermined, and it is 
  541: currently unknown whether tirzepatide causes medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) in humans.34 
  542: 
  543: 
  544: === PAGE BREAK ===
  545: 
  546: Nonetheless, exercising maximum caution, tirzepatide is strictly contraindicated in patients with 
  547: a personal or family history of MTC, as well as in patients diagnosed with Multiple Endocrine 
  548: Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2), a genetic condition that heavily predisposes individuals to 
  549: MTC.34 
  550: Regulatory bodies currently assert that the clinical value of routine monitoring via serum 
  551: calcitonin or thyroid ultrasound for early detection is uncertain.34 Instead, standard of care 
  552: dictates that patients must be thoroughly counseled on recognizing mass-related symptoms, 
  553: such as a physical lump or swelling in the neck, persistent hoarseness, chronic trouble 
  554: swallowing (dysphagia), or unexplained shortness of breath (dyspnea).34 
  555:  
  556:  
  557:  
  558: Pediatric and Geriatric Considerations 
  559: Clinical data regarding specific demographics continues to evolve. For geriatric populations 
  560: (patients aged 65 and older), extensive clinical trial data indicates no overall differences in the 
  561: safety or efficacy of tirzepatide compared to younger adult cohorts, allowing for standard 
  562: prescribing practices in elderly populations.2 
  563: 
  564: 
  565: === PAGE BREAK ===
  566: 
  567: In pediatric populations, the data is highly restricted. While the Zepbound brand (marketed 
  568: specifically for chronic weight management) currently states that no data is available and 
  569: efficacy has not been established for pediatric patients under 18 years of age 37, the Mounjaro 
  570: formulation (indicated for type 2 diabetes) carries specific FDA label allowances for pediatric

  418: safety profiles have proven exceptionally favorable, culminating in landmark trial data. 
  419: The SURPASS-CVOT Trial: A New Benchmark in Cardioprotection 
  420: The SURPASS-CVOT (Cardiovascular Outcomes Trial) stands as a monumental milestone in 
  421: incretin literature. Published in late 2025 in the New England Journal of Medicine, it is notable 
  422: for being the first large-scale cardiovascular outcome trial to utilize an active comparator 
  423: rather than a placebo.28 The double-blind trial randomized 13,299 patients with type 2 diabetes 
  424: and established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) to receive either tirzepatide or 
  425: dulaglutide.30 Dulaglutide is a GLP-1 receptor agonist that had previously demonstrated 
  426: significant cardiovascular benefit against placebo, establishing an exceptionally high bar for 
  427: comparison.29 
  428: Over a median follow-up of 4 years, the primary composite endpoint—Major Adverse 
  429: Cardiovascular Events (MACE-3), defined as death from cardiovascular causes, nonfatal 
  430: myocardial infarction, or nonfatal stroke—occurred in 12.2% (801 patients) of the tirzepatide 
  431: group versus 13.1% (862 patients) of the dulaglutide group.30 This outcome achieved strict 
  432: statistical criteria for non-inferiority (Hazard Ratio 0.92; 95.3% CI, 0.83 to 1.01; P = 0.003) and 
  433: trended very closely toward superiority (P = 0.09).30 
  434: Given that dulaglutide already significantly reduces MACE relative to placebo, proving 
  435: non-inferiority against it—and demonstrating numerical superiority across almost all

docs\broad_dr_domain_general_plan.md:120:**The single biggest risk to a competitive broad report:** the quantified trade-off (§1.2 / §4 "loses"). Fixing routing, backends, tiering, sections, and verification (Steps 1-5) makes POLARIS *produce a real policy report*, but matching ChatGPT's defensible numeric break-even + sensitivity table is a genuine CAPABILITY POLARIS does not have today (its contract architecture models clinical trial entities, not cost/sensitivity curves). If the re-parameter stops at "domain-general retrieval + general sections" and does not add a quantitative-synthesis path the Writer can execute from retrieved inputs, POLARIS will produce a faithful but qualitatively-thin report that still loses to the frontier on the exact dimension (the quantified trade-off) that makes a broad analyst report decision-useful. That capability gap, not the four narrowness gaps, is what should worry the spend decision most.

docs\clinical_rag_sota_deepest_research_2026_05_27.md:28:**The single most expensive belief POLARIS is currently holding that this research overturns:** the operator-locked DeepSeek V4 Pro generator is the WRONG generator for the hallucination-sensitivity dimension. docs\full_power_polaris_architecture_2026_05_31.md:35:**Stage 1 — Scope & decomposition (PICO faceting).** Detects domain (clinical / regulatory / policy), parses the question into Population / Intervention / Comparator / Outcome plus the safety facets clinical RAG requires — contraindications, adverse-event signals, special populations, regulatory-label divergence, comparator head-to-heads — and sets a per-domain `relevance_tau` floor. **Model/tool:** existing scope gate (`src/polaris_graph/nodes/`, §9.2) + Writer for PICO + synonym/MeSH-candidate proposal, validated against the keyless NCBI E-utilities MeSH database so no hallucinated MeSH ID reaches a query. **Fixes vs shipped:** `query_decomposer.py` is pure string-splitting — no LLM, no MeSH, no PICO, no synonyms, hard cap 6 sub-queries (`query_decomposer.py:33-36,108-148`); it cannot expand "tirzepatide"→"Mounjaro" or map "T2DM"→MeSH `Diabetes Mellitus, Type 2`.   180: 
  181: def load_report_contract_for_slug(
  182:     template: dict[str, Any] | None,
  183:     slug: str,
  184: ) -> ReportContract | None:
  185:     """M-54 public accessor: load the resolved report contract for
  186:     one research-question slug from a loaded scope template.
  187: 
  188:     Returns None when the template has no contract for this slug
  189:     (backwards-compatible: V28/V29 runs continue to work without
  190:     contract).
  191: 
  192:     Raises ContractSchemaError with precise path on any schema
  193:     malformation.
  194: 
  195:     Used by M-55 frame compiler. Not called directly by generator
  196:     stages — those consume the resolved ReportContract object.
  197:     """
  198:     if not isinstance(template, dict):
  199:         return None
  200:     if not isinstance(slug, str) or not slug.strip():
  201:         return None
  202:     by_slug = template.get("per_query_report_contract")
  203:     if by_slug is None:
  204:         return None
  205:     if not isinstance(by_slug, dict):
  206:         raise ContractSchemaError(
  207:             "per_query_report_contract",
  208:             f"expected dict, got {type(by_slug).__name__}",
  209:         )
  210:     raw = by_slug.get(slug)
  211:     if raw is None:
  212:         return None
  213:     if not isinstance(raw, dict):
  214:         raise ContractSchemaError(
  215:             f"per_query_report_contract.{slug}",
  216:             f"expected dict, got {type(raw).__name__}",
  217:         )
  218: 
  219:     # ── schema_version ────────────────────────────────────────────
  220:     schema_version = raw.get("schema_version")
  221:     if schema_version is None:
  222:         raise ContractSchemaError(
  223:             f"per_query_report_contract.{slug}.schema_version",
  224:             "missing required field",
  225:         )
  226:     if not isinstance(schema_version, str):
  227:         raise ContractSchemaError(
  228:             f"per_query_report_contract.{slug}.schema_version",
  229:             f"expected string, got {type(schema_version).__name__}",
  230:         )
  231:     # Forward-compat: warn (don't raise) on unknown schema version
  232:     # via logger in M-55 compiler. At loader level we accept.
  233: 
  234:     # ── required_entities ─────────────────────────────────────────
  235:     entities_raw = raw.get("required_entities")
  236:     if not isinstance(entities_raw, list):
  237:         raise ContractSchemaError(
  238:             f"per_query_report_contract.{slug}.required_entities",
  239:             f"expected list, got {type(entities_raw).__name__}",
  240:         )
  241:     if not entities_raw:
  242:         raise ContractSchemaError(
  243:             f"per_query_report_contract.{slug}.required_entities",
  244:             "must contain at least one entity",
  245:         )
  246:     entities: list[RequiredEntity] = []
  247:     entity_yaml_index: dict[str, int] = {}  # entity id -> YAML list index
  248:     seen_entity_ids: set[str] = set()
  249:     for i, e in enumerate(entities_raw):
  250:         path = f"per_query_report_contract.{slug}.required_entities[{i}]"
  251:         if not isinstance(e, dict):
  252:             raise ContractSchemaError(
  253:                 path, f"expected dict, got {type(e).__name__}"
  254:             )
  255:         missing = _REQUIRED_ENTITY_KEYS - set(e.keys())
  256:         if missing:
  257:             raise ContractSchemaError(
  258:                 path, f"missing required keys: {sorted(missing)}"
  259:             )
  260:         # id must be unique within contract

    1: """I-arch-001b — synthesize a v30.1 per_query_report_contract from a v6 template.
    2: 
    3: Input: v6 template JSON (config/v6_templates/<template_id>.json), query_slug.
    4: Output: {query_slug: <v30.1 contract dict>} ready to merge into a scope
    5: template's `per_query_report_contract` key, where M-55 frame_compiler and
    6: load_report_contract_for_slug both consume it.
    7: 
    8: Per Codex APPROVE iter 5: every entity carries a concrete http(s) url_pattern
    9: from T1 + a stable anchor; this satisfies M-56's `_fetch_url_pattern()` which
   10: treats the value as a literal URL, not a regex.
   11: """
   12: 
   13: from __future__ import annotations
   14: 
   15: import hashlib
   16: from typing import Any
   17: 
   18: # Per-template defaults for the type discriminator + identifier field names.
   19: # Codex iter-2: rolled clinical→drug/condition into a generic "frame" entity-
   20: # type so the v30 compiler doesn't try to dispatch on type-specific paths
   21: # that don't exist for synthesized contracts.
   22: _TYPE_FOR_TEMPLATE: dict[str, str] = {
   23:     "clinical": "drug",
   24:     "policy": "policy",
   25:     "tech": "policy",
   26:     "due_diligence": "policy",
   27:     "ai_sovereignty": "policy",
   28:     "canada_us": "policy",
   29:     "workforce": "policy",
   30:     "custom": "policy",
   31: }
   32: 
   33: _REQUIRED_FIELDS_FOR_TEMPLATE: dict[str, list[str]] = {
   34:     "clinical": ["rxcui", "drugbank_id", "icd_10"],
   35:     "policy": ["jurisdiction", "policy_id", "year"],
   36:     "tech": ["jurisdiction", "technology_id", "year"],
   37:     "due_diligence": ["entity_id", "jurisdiction", "year"],
   38:     "ai_sovereignty": ["bill_id", "jurisdiction", "year"],
   39:     "canada_us": ["jurisdiction", "treaty_id", "year"],
   40:     "workforce": ["jurisdiction", "indicator_id", "year"],
   41:     "custom": ["jurisdiction", "topic_id", "year"],
   42: }
   43: 
   44: # Last-resort URL when a template has empty T1. Per Codex iter-5 P2 accepted-
   45: # remaining: this is acceptable for v1 (all 8 current templates have T1);
   46: # future templates should fail-loud, deferred to follow-up.
   47: _EMPTY_T1_FALLBACK = "https://www.canada.ca/en/research.html"
   48: 
   49: 
   50: def _anchor_for(template_id: str, frame_id: str, query_slug: str) -> str:
   51:     """Stable, unique anchor: <template>:<frame>:<slug_prefix>:<sha256_8>.
   52: 
   53:     Used as a secondary stable id (not the retrieval locator — url_pattern is).
   54:     Collision-safe for any realistic curator workload (2^32 combinations).
   55:     """
   56:     h = hashlib.sha256(
   57:         f"{template_id}|{frame_id}|{query_slug}".encode("utf-8")
   58:     ).hexdigest()[:8]
   59:     return f"{template_id}:{frame_id}:{query_slug[:40]}:{h}"
   60: 
   61: 
   62: def _concrete_url_for_entity(frame_idx: int, source_tiers: dict[str, Any]) -> str:
   63:     """Concrete http(s) URL for this entity, rotated through T1 by frame index.
   64: 
   65:     M-56's `_fetch_url_pattern()` treats this value as a literal URL, NOT a
   66:     regex (per Codex iter-4 P1 verification at frame_fetcher.py:780,1046).
   67:     Returning a regex would fail to fetch.
   68: 
   69:     Rotation: entity[i] uses T1[i % len(T1)]. Different frames retrieve
   70:     different roots; strict_verify later filters per-frame relevance.
   71:     """
   72:     t1 = source_tiers.get("T1") or []
   73:     if t1:
   74:         return t1[frame_idx % len(t1)]
   75:     return _EMPTY_T1_FALLBACK
   76: 
   77: 
   78: def build_v30_contract(
   79:     v6_template: dict[str, Any],
   80:     query_slug: str,
   81:     question: str | None = None,
   82: ) -> dict[str, Any]:
   83:     """Synthesize a v30.1 per_query_report_contract.{query_slug} dict from a v6 template.
   84: 
   85:     Args:
   86:         v6_template: loaded v6 template JSON (config/v6_templates/<id>.json)
   87:         query_slug: pipeline-A URL-safe slug (e.g. "clinical_glp1_t2dm")
   88:         question: reserved for future question-aware seeding; iter-1 implementation
   89:                   uses frame_id as entity_id basis.
   90: 
   91:     Returns:
   92:         {query_slug: <contract>} where <contract> has schema_version="v30.1",
   93:         required_entities, rendering_slots (dict-keyed by slot_id), and
   94:         section_order. Round-trips clean via load_report_contract_for_slug.
   95:     """
   96:     del question  # reserved; suppress unused-arg warning
   97:     template_id = v6_template.get("template_id", "")
   98:     frame_manifest = v6_template.get("frame_manifest") or []
   99:     source_tiers = v6_template.get("source_tiers") or {}
  100: 
  101:     entity_type = _TYPE_FOR_TEMPLATE.get(template_id, "policy")
  102:     required_fields = _REQUIRED_FIELDS_FOR_TEMPLATE.get(
  103:         template_id, ["jurisdiction", "year"]
  104:     )
  105: 
  106:     required_entities: list[dict[str, Any]] = []
  107:     rendering_slots: dict[str, dict[str, Any]] = {}
  108:     sections_seen: list[str] = []  # ordered, deduped
  109: 
  110:     for idx, frame in enumerate(frame_manifest):
  111:         frame_id = frame.get("frame_id", f"frame_{idx}")
  112:         frame_name = frame.get("frame_name", frame_id.replace("_", " ").title())
  113:         slot_id = f"{frame_id}_slot"
  114:         section = _section_for_frame(template_id, frame_id, idx)
  115:         if section not in sections_seen:
  116:             sections_seen.append(section)
  117: 
  118:         rendering_slots[slot_id] = {
  119:             "section": section,
  120:             "subsection_title": frame_name,
  121:             "ordering": idx + 1,
  122:             "required": True,
  123:         }
  124:         required_entities.append({
  125:             "id": f"{frame_id}_entity",
  126:             "type": entity_type,
  127:             "required_fields": list(required_fields),
  128:             "min_fields_for_completion": 1,
  129:             "rendering_slot": slot_id,
  130:             "url_pattern": _concrete_url_for_entity(idx, source_tiers),
  131:             "anchor": _anchor_for(template_id, frame_id, query_slug),
  132:         })
  133: 
  134:     contract = {
  135:         "schema_version": "v30.1",
  136:         "required_entities": required_entities,
  137:         "rendering_slots": rendering_slots,
  138:         "section_order": sections_seen,
  139:     }
  140:     return {query_slug: contract}
  141: 
  142: 
  143: def _section_for_frame(template_id: str, frame_id: str, idx: int) -> str:
  144:     """Group frames into 2-4 sections per template. Mechanical grouping; per-
  145:     template overrides can be added later via report_contract_override blocks.
  146:     """
  147:     # Per Codex iter-5 P2: section grouping is left mechanical-but-stable;
  148:     # template-specific overrides are a follow-up.
  149:     if template_id == "clinical":
  150:         section_map = {
  151:             "efficacy": "Efficacy",
  152:             "safety": "Safety",
  153:             "labelling": "Regulatory",
  154:             "post_market": "Post-market",
  155:             "subgroup": "Subgroups",
  156:         }
  157:     else:
  158:         # Policy templates default: split into Background / Findings / Implications.
  159:         if idx < 2:
  160:             return "Background"
  161:         if idx < 4:
  162:             return "Findings"
  163:         return "Implications"
  164:     return section_map.get(frame_id, "Other")

# AI sovereignty scope template — Carney delivery (I-tpl-006).
#
# Used when the user is researching AI sovereignty: data residency,
# compute sovereignty, model provenance, export controls, and the
# regulatory regimes governing AI development across jurisdictions.
# The corpus should be dominated by primary regulatory documents
# (T3), national AI strategy white papers (T3), and credible policy
# commentary (T6). Industry whitepapers are admissible as T5 with
# strong skepticism (frequently marketing-driven).

domain: ai_sovereignty
description: >
  Sovereign-AI corpus emphasizing primary regulatory documents,
  national AI strategies, export-control regimes, and credible
  policy commentary on data + compute + model sovereignty.

inclusion_criteria:
  - "National AI strategies (Canada, EU, UK, US, Japan, Singapore, France, Germany, Israel, India, China where translatable)."
  - "Regulatory documents on AI — EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, Canada AIDA, US Executive Orders on AI, China interim measures on generative AI, UK AI Safety Institute outputs."
  - "Export-control documents — BIS rules on advanced compute, Wassenaar Arrangement updates, EU dual-use regulation revisions."
  - "Data-residency law — GDPR plus national addenda, PIPEDA, PIPL, LGPD, sectoral health-data laws."
  - "Compute-sovereignty / sovereign-cloud frameworks — Gaia-X, Bharat Cloud, OVH Sovereign, MS / AWS / GCP sovereign-region documentation (treated as T5 — vendor/industry source)."
  - "Peer-reviewed AI-policy journal articles (treated as T1)."
  - "Policy-institute reports from Brookings, RAND, Belfer Center, AI Now Institute, GovAI (treated as T6 — analyst commentary, NOT peer-reviewed)."
  - "Government technology-strategy committee reports."

exclusion_criteria:
  - "Vendor whitepapers presented as primary regulatory interpretation."
  - "Op-eds without disclosed affiliation in general-interest media."
  - "Crypto / Web3 sovereignty claims framed as AI sovereignty (different regulatory regime; cite separately if relevant)."

expected_tier_distribution:
  - tier: T3
    min_fraction: 0.35
    max_fraction: 0.65
    rationale: >
      Regulatory documents (AI Act, executive orders, export controls)
      and national AI strategies are the primary evidence base.
  - tier: T6
    min_fraction: 0.10
    max_fraction: 0.30
